attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of progressive shortness of breath and daytime sleepiness. She has a medical history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and multiple sclerosis. Her multiple sclerosis has been well controlled, although she has some left hand weakness and lower limb sensory changes. She takes metformin, lisinopril, and cladribine. She has a 10 pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago. On examination, she appears drowsy with a respiratory rate of 22/min. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 145/90 mmHg, SpO2 88% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her weight is 135 kg and height is 165 cm (BMI 49.6 kg/m2). Chest examination reveals decreased breath sounds bilaterally. She has mild pedal oedema.

Arterial blood gas on room air shows: pH 7.35 (7.35-7.45), PaCO2 52 mmHg (35-45), PaO2 55 mmHg (80-100), HCO3- 28 mEq/L (22-26).

Lung function tests show:

  • FEV1: 65% predicted (>80%)

  • FVC: 70% predicted (>80%)

  • FEV1/FVC ratio: 0.75 (>0.7)

  • TLC: 75% predicted (80-120)

  • RV: 80% predicted (75-120)

  • DLCO: 85% predicted (80-120)

Polysomnography results:

  • Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI): 4 events/hour (<5)

  • Oxygen desaturation index: 70 events/hour (<5)

  • Lowest SpO2: 72% (>90%)

  • Time spent with SpO2 <90%: 65% of total sleep time (<5%)

  • End-tidal CO2 >45 mmHg for 70% of total sleep time (<25%)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis for this patient is obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS). OHS is characterized by the triad of obesity (BMI >30 kg/m2), daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 >45 mmHg), and sleep-disordered breathing. This patient meets all these criteria with a BMI of 49.6 kg/m2, daytime PaCO2 of 52 mmHg, and severe sleep-disordered breathing evidenced by the polysomnography results, despite a low Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI).

The lung function tests support this diagnosis. The mildly reduced FEV1 and FVC with a normal FEV1/FVC ratio suggest a restrictive pattern, which is consistent with obesity. The slightly reduced Total Lung Capacity (TLC) further supports this restrictive pattern. The normal Diffusing Capacity for Carbon Monoxide (DLCO) indicates that gas exchange at the alveolar level is not significantly impaired, which is typical in OHS.

The pathophysiology of OHS is complex and multifactorial. Obesity leads to increased mechanical load on the respiratory system, reducing lung volumes and chest wall compliance. This results in increased work of breathing and reduced ventilatory efficiency. Additionally, leptin resistance in obesity may impair central respiratory drive. During sleep, these factors are exacerbated, leading to hypoventilation and CO2 retention. Over time, renal compensation occurs, resulting in chronic hypercapnia and mild respiratory acidosis.

Importantly, while about 90% of patients with OHS also have significant obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA), approximately 10% do not. This patient falls into the latter category, as evidenced by the low AHI of 4 events/hour. Patients with OHS without significant OSA tend to be older and are more likely to be female, which fits this patient's profile. Despite the absence of obstructive events, the patient still exhibits severe sleep-disordered breathing, as shown by the frequent oxygen desaturations and prolonged hypercapnia during sleep.

Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) can cause hypercapnia, and the patient does have a history of smoking. However, the lung function tests show only mild obstruction (FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.75), which is not typical for significant COPD. The normal DLCO also argues against significant COPD. The severe obesity, sleep study results, and degree of hypercapnia are more consistent with OHS than COPD.

Heart failure can cause shortness of breath and sleep-disordered breathing (central sleep apnea), and the patient does have mild pedal oedema. However, heart failure does not typically cause daytime hypercapnia. The absence of other significant signs of heart failure makes this diagnosis less likely than OHS.

Neuromuscular disease can cause hypoventilation, and the patient does have multiple sclerosis. However, her multiple sclerosis is well controlled, and while she has some left hand weakness and lower limb sensory changes, multiple sclerosis rarely causes significant respiratory muscle weakness leading to hypoventilation. The presence of severe obesity and sleep-disordered breathing makes OHS a much more likely diagnosis than respiratory complications of multiple sclerosis.

In conclusion, while this patient has a history of smoking and multiple sclerosis, which could potentially contribute to respiratory issues, the overall clinical picture is most consistent with OHS. The presence of severe obesity, daytime hypercapnia, and sleep-disordered breathing, despite a low AHI, strongly supports the diagnosis of OHS. The lung function tests show a restrictive pattern with preserved gas exchange, which is typical for OHS. Management typically involves weight loss, nocturnal non-invasive ventilation, and treatment of comorbidities.
learningPoints: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome is characterised by obesity (BMI >30 kg/m2), daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 >45 mmHg), and sleep-disordered breathing.
Polysomnography is essential in diagnosing obesity hypoventilation syndrome and differentiating it from obstructive sleep apnoea.
While most patients with obesity hypoventilation syndrome also have obstructive sleep apnoea, a small subset (about 10%) do not, and these patients tend to be older and female.
references: de Athayde RAB, de Oliveira JRB, Lorenzi G, Genta PR. Obesity hypoventilation syndrome: a current review. J Bras Pneumol. 2018;44(6):510–8.
Masa JF, Pépin JL, Borel JC, Mokhlesi B, Murphy PB, Sánchez-Quiroga MÁ. Obesity hypoventilation syndrome. Eur Respir Rev. 2019 Mar 31;28(151):180097.
